sentences.text: Dr.
Henry Oster.
When I wore the knickers, the opportunity to hide seeds that we were supposed to plant was, of course, an incredible temptation.
And under the risk of being beaten or killed or sent out of the ghetto, I would fill up those knickers with peas or beans.
But you had to go through a nightly German inspection.
And my major concern was that I couldn't walk at a normal gait because those seeds would rattle.
So somebody once asked, how come you're walking so slowly?
I said, well, I'm very tired.
But it really was to get those things.
And the one thing you'll always remember-- not the seeds, not the knickers-- all you remember is your heart beating because your life was at stake, at least potentially.
Somebody did get caught and received a severe beating.
People were actually, for if not similar infractions, but somewhat close to it-- lying, stealing, other things-- were actually hanged at a public hanging, which everybody had to attend.
And the place that I worked had the great distinction that two of my coworkers were the executioners that did the hanging.
So I always thought that maybe, just maybe, if anything should ever happen, I'd have a friend at the gallows, you know?
Wasn't so humorous at that time.
They provided my mother and me with a very minimal extra food because the total food portions that were doled out was a occasional can of horse meat.
We did get a loaf of bread for the week per person.
And at work, soups were provided.
And that was your main food.
You scrounged.
You ate everything from leaves to bark to potato peels, whatever.
A luxury, and I do mean a luxury, was when you could go to the German kitchen and get the, the coffee-- what do you call it?
What's left over from coffee-- coffee grind.
Because they did not use regular coffee.
They used ersatz coffee, which was mostly roasted barley which was still at least hopefully of some nutritional value.
And you'd make, not hamburgers, but patties.
I mean, you'd make patties like hamburger out of this.
And eating this and eliminating it was not a joy.
But you tried everything and anything to get some nutritional value.
We were there till 1944 when my mother and I were supposedly given the privilege of staying behind to bring in the harvest.
Now, from 1940-- I'm sorry '43, not '44-- we were supposed to have the identification card, an identification badge, that would allow us to stay behind.
Well, what happened was that during the time that we were in this ghetto, you had this tremendous, tremendous fear that you would be caught in a raid.
Now, raids were the standard way of slowly cleaning, clearing out the ghetto.
You lived in a apartment building sort of a structure.
And you had a periodic raid where the street was cordoned off-- always at night, always a German shepherd, always with a searchlight, always with sticks and bayonets, even.
And they would come into the room and clear it out, chase you down the staircase, if there was one, onto the trucks for a destination, again, unknown.
We happened to live in a place that had sort of an attic, at least the-- underneath the roof, which had to hold up the roof some major beams.
And the door would open up to our room.
And I came up with the idea that if I could step up onto the door handle, I could get through the opening, get underneath the, the attic.
This is what we had done twice.
And we went as far away against the eve of the, the roof where it joined the-- the floor.

And by being very skinny, very tiny-- both my mother and I, by now were very, very underweight-- we were flat enough that when the Germans stood up on the door handle to look in, he yelled out, there's nobody up here.
And we managed to escape two raids two times, both times.
Finally, the time had come where they said, you have to bring in the harvest.
And in order to have the privilege to do this, you need a special permit.

And those of you who are in the agriculture department and are responsible for that, you and any coworker-- or if you have a family member-- are to report to a place that was a very large area between two barracks.
The barracks was the German administration.
We-- I called my mother from the street-- no phones-- calling, come on down.
We are going to get a permit.
And she doesn't understand that.
It was late at night again.
She was home from work.
And because it had to be done in a hurry.
We presented ourselves.
We stood there in the darkness.
And suddenly-- and we're waiting to get this permit or this-- whatever identification it was supposed to have been.
We stood there for a few minutes.
They brought more and more people.
And suddenly, lights went on on the building, illuminating all of us.
And the shutters on the doors-- on the doors and the shutters on the windows from the barracks fell down, kind of automatically.
And in each window, you had a machine gun or soldiers pointing a gun.
You were in a trap.
We had no opportunity to go back for clothing of which we had-- We had no way to get back for food.
That's a joke.
There was no food.
You were trapped right then and there.
And you were taken to a railroad facility, which was not too far away.
We marched there.
And we were shoved into the proverbial cattle cars, open cars with a window that was covered by barbed wire so nobody would even be able to get out through this tiny window.
The cattle cars were covered.
You couldn't climb over it.
They were just simply closed.
And there was no consideration given to how many people were packed in.
And you took off to, again, destination unknown.
This was in August 1943.
You respond?
You don't respond.
You don't respond in-- with emotions.
You don't respond-- except maybe fear because, again, you don't know what's going on.
I was terribly upset because I had been trapped because it was supposed to be that we had the permission to stay.
Because at least you knew what you had then and there.
You'd made it for already two, two and a half years.
You felt that you were not safe, but at least you escaped repeatedly.
You felt you had whatever you believe in, something, someone watch over you to have gotten along this far.
We drove for about two days and arrived in a place that was completely barren.
There was nothing here.
Because everybody took a look out there.
We did have, of course, some people pass away.
We didn't want to even discuss hygiene, personal needs.
I mean, it was-- it was awful.
Well, what was so awful is the, the feeling that everybody was deathly afraid because we knew we were not going to paradise.
We knew that we were leaving a place that was horrible, but there's normally no way that you think you can get to anything better.
We had no idea what was going on worldwide.
At that point, we certainly had no idea what had been invaded, what was happening, Allies-- nothing.
We knew we were in Poland, and we were on on a train from one hell to another.
We had many people who lost their faith and cried, moaned.
A lot of people were panicking.
Some people talked about suicide.
They're not going to get-- all kinds of things.
But we did have in that day and a half or two days a certain attrition where people passed away.
And the family members were there.
I mean, you just can't describe what goes on in the confinement of a small car packed with people.
I thought that was packed till later.
Then we stood there on this, this empty place.
You could look out, saw absolutely nothing.
We didn't know where we were.
And the train stayed there until darkness, which, of course, once again, welcome to German mentality in waiting for darkness.
When the train moved, it moved an extremely short distance.
But because you could only see sideways-- you couldn't look forward-- we arrived at a huge railroad platform.
The doors were ripped open.
We were yanked out, pushed out, beaten up.
You can't describe it.
Always in German, [GERMAN], hurry, hurry, hurry.
The German shepherds were barking.
They were biting.
And we noticed immediately that an attempt was made to separate men and women.
I stayed on the right side.
And all I can remember is my mother, sort of like a hurricane, had taken away, just whooshed away visually, just simply disappeared into a crowd.
By now, of course, you know, when you're 15 years old, you just simply can't understand what's going on for that moment.
And you certainly don't have a life's philosophy to figure things out.
You were helpless.
You were confused.
And of course, you were in the crowd of men.
And the women were on the other side.
And in a tremendously cruel manner, we were forced to march forward.
We marched forward to a building in front of which was an officer, an SS officer, on a-- on a box of two steps, three steps.
And he looked at you, and he pointed with the swagger stick that-- of the men, you go to the right, and the other go to the left.
Meantime, of course, you're looking for your mother, and everybody's looking for family.
Occasionally they would see it and wave or recognize, but it was absolute chaos, confusion.
It was the ultimate in panic, which is exactly what the Germans want.
I went to the right, as directed, and many years later found out that was the infamous Dr.
Mengele who was making a selection.
And you remember the face but no great detail, except when history shows you these things.
And I was shoved into an adjacent building.
And the first thing is we had to take our clothes off.
We had to submit to get our hair shaved off.
We were shoved through a room, a facility-- not a hallway-- it's a very large room that had a, a shower of excruciating solution fall on you.
And it especially hurt the head that had just been shaven, which as it turned out was a disinfection solution.
And then there was a shower, a real shower, and no towels or anything like this.
But in the fore room before you went into the shower, they asked, has anybody been a policeman in the ghetto?
And there were some.
They were taken apart-- aside, and they were immediately beaten to death.
Now, we thought that's a punishment.
No, the camp police, which-- we didn't know where we were at that time-- did not want competition, and so beatings were right in front of us.
That was really one of the earliest times I had seen murders right in front of me.
In the ghetto, you saw a lot of dead people and beatings and hangings, but you didn't see blatant murders right in front of you.
We were shoved out of the room wet.

And as we walked out the door, the packages came flying at you, which was nothing more than a bundle of white and blue striped clothing with a pair of clophoppers, which are just wooden shoes-- no socks, no underwear, nothing.
And you were told, grab it-- not a question of size or anything.
We're sitting there shivering.
This was August.
It was already cold in the place we were.
We didn't know yet where we were.
And as we walked out to this place after the shower, we walked out into this incredible strange illumination, which came from stacks.
When we had gotten there, we could not exactly see what was going on, but there was a peculiar odor, but, you know, the building was tall.
The facilities were such that you couldn't see what was going on.
When we got out to the place trying to dry ourselves with the clothing we just had, here were camp police, or kapos, as they were, which were inmates.
And with batons, they beat you-- rush, rush, hurry.
Move over there.
Do this.
Do that.
And they said, welcome to Auschwitz.
That was the first time that we knew where we had arrived.
After all this procedure and processing, that's the first time that we knew.
Well, it wasn't actually Auschwitz.

Auschwitz was really the total name for the railroad facility that existed long before World War II which happened to be, just by coincidence, the geographical confluence of many, many European countries' railroad facilities.
And that's why the Germans chose this.
From every country they occupied-- including Germany, Poland, whatever-- railroads would go there.
Nobody understood why.
It was a sleepy little town of nothing, just a railroad facility.
Well, now I was in Birkenau.
Birkenau, I was in the camp C, which is-- they just had A, B, and C.
They did have something.
They called it Zigeunerlager, which was for non-Jews, political prisoners, or gypsy, which is what Zigeuner means.
We had no idea what would happen.
We had no idea what we were there for because we had never heard about Auschwitz or Birkenau or anything in all these years.

To us, it was just another camp, maybe another ghetto, except when you looked around and you see barracks after barracks after barracks and people languishing there and just laying around, sitting there.
That was just a very strange impression you had.
And you did wonder what those uniforms meant.
But I had heard about Buchenwald.
I'd heard about Dachau from people and one distant cousin that had come back in 1939 or actually, early 1940, but I had no idea what this was.
So people were terribly confused.
People were terribly intimidated because it was only a few minutes later that we realized what this was all about because we were told from the other inmates that this was an extermination camp.
And we-- At this point, had you already received a number?
No.
You don't receive a number going in.
I'll tell you in a little bit why, why the number.

So now we realize that the chance of seeing your mother again, whom I only knew with gray hair and when we were so emaciated from the ghetto, to look at this woman and make her appear to be worthy to survive, to be put to forced labor, was basically none.
The concept was that this was a camp where ultimately you were to be exterminated but predominantly, because they were a German industry, to be used for forced labor.
Yes.
I stayed at camp for about two months.
Nothing.
You just laid around.
The cruelty the Germans exhibited every morning and every night was called appell, which was a head count where you had to stand there.
And the Germans would have a great deal of fun hosing you down with water in the cold of the wintertime, making-- having games that one doesn't really want to know about.

But one of the cruel ones which I had to not participate, fortunately-- I wouldn't be here-- had to witness was they thought it was very funny to take prisoners, send them around-- stand around the circle, and have one prisoner selected to have a bowel movement in the middle of these people.
And most human beings cannot do this.
And so the game was to force you to do something that's instinctual or reflex-wise not easy, and your life depended on it.
Well, those are the few things that-- I mean, the cruelty was unbelievable.
But actual activity was none.
You just wait for your fate.
We had been there for a little bit more than two months.
And the rule that everybody sort of assumed was that you don't ever volunteer.
It was strange.
I was in the latrine, which in itself is ridiculous because with one thin slice of bread and a little bit of stew, you had very little reason to go using the toilet facilities.
Somebody comes in, and they're looking for volunteers.
No way in the world will I ever understand what made me jump up, run to the place where they're making a selection, and not only volunteer, but I raised my arms, and I speak German.
I speak German.
No idea.
The German officer that made the selection understood and said, OK.
We were 131 juveniles, exclusively juveniles, that were now taken to a barrack, and now we were given a number.
There was no need for a number to be given when you go in because the extermination camp isn't interested in numbering you to be killed.
They need to give you a number, they felt, in case you might escape.
Now, you looked like a skeleton.
And you looked like something from outer space because you had no hair.
You're certainly not going to be inconspicuous in a crowd of people if you should escape.
And escape was just simply not possible.
That simply-- that is just a physical impossibility.
The surrounding camp and then a perimeter of additional fortification just wasn't a way to escape.
I was given B-7648.
Sure.
I was put on a truck, two trucks, about 60 people, 60 children, you know, less room.
And we drove approximately five, six, maybe 10 minutes.

And from Birkenau, we were then transported and released in the actual concentration camp of Auschwitz, which was a separate and distinct, different, real concentration camp, versus the extermination facility of Birkenau.
The smell and the odor, of course, all these crematorium that were working full time.
But in Auschwitz, in the camp, there was no crematorium.
There was nothing there intended to kill.
Beatings, yes, but no systematic-- that was done the five kilometers or whatever distance it was.
When we arrived there, we found out that we had each to share bunks, while in Birkenau, we just laid on the floor.
So you didn't have anything.
And this was quite a step up, and you felt like you have a place to sleep.
They were three bunks high on straw, no pillows, and some rags, which if you want to call it blankets, it's a misnomer, but it's-- lice-infested, vermin all over the place.
But it was something else than from what we had come.

It was only in the early '60s, maybe the late '50s, that I found out through historical research that in all the years till 1945, only two transports, a total of two, of juveniles ever left Birkenau for something that wasn't for extermination-- 165 before I went and 131 that I was in.

And the trucks that were used to transport from Birkenau to Auschwitz were exactly the same trucks that you could put the carbon monoxide exhaust into the van, into the truck, drive around for approximately a little bit longer to suffocate everybody, and then, bam.
So there were only 296 juveniles that ever actually, in an organized fashion, were selected and literally taken out for something other than extermination.
Of course, we didn't know that at the time.
I was in Auschwitz.
And my responsibility, with all 130 others, was to propagate horses, because Germany needed horsepower rather than motorized power for many of the functions in occupied countries and transportation.
And they wanted to have horses as quickly as possible.
So it turns out that of all the 131, there were only two of us who spoke German.
Consequently, even in the ghetto, being a [GERMAN], a German Jew, was not a very pleasant situation if the only language you speak is that of your enemy.
And so I learned Yiddish very quickly.
But I still, of course, even to this day, speak German.
So the question had come up that our responsibility was to take care of four horses each, cleaning the stable, and be responsible to mate them.

Well, the problem was that the stallion, which was a very vicious but a gorgeous horse by the name of Baba Rosa, was my responsibility because the son of a gun understood German, and I was the only one that spoke the language.
So whenever I say, come here, do this, that doggone horse would understand it.
Now, my biggest problem was to help a horse to mate is not your every day kick in the pants activity.
It's dangerous.
The horse is wild.
I got repeatedly cut from the horse tail because it's as sharp as blades.
And the Germans thought it was funny to have this little Jew get kicked around by the horse trying to help him to mate.
And at least it did give me one opportunity.
That horse and my other four received better food than we did.
And we had access to the food, whether it were oats or some carrots, sometimes raw potatoes, whatever it is.
And you had the extra food that allowed you to survive.

The only humor that I ever encountered in four and a half years in camps was at that particular place when it was a rule that the little horses, once they were born, were not to have any water, only mother's milk.
And when nobody was looking, which sometimes was possible, all these horses were taken to the water trough, and now accompanied by the little ones.
It was an enormous commo-- it was dusty.
It was unbelieve-- we let the little horses drink, which of course caused them to have what the Germans thought was an epidemic of diarrhea.
Now, it wasn't done, believe me, to make fun of the Germans.
It wasn't done to have any joke or sense of humor.
It had a very serious reason.
They wouldn't nurse.
And we were then forced to milk the horses.
And instead of having to go on the ground, [POINTS] it went into us.
And so we had horse's milk, a very important nutritional bonus other people didn't have.
The number of hours we worked-- 16 to 18 per day.
We had four to six hours of rest.
We had to march half an hour.
But we had, at least, a life that kept us away from the selection to be exterminated.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I didn't know we had that much.
